ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for the specific composite dish of crispy samgyeopsal with scrambled eggs and rice. FOODON_00002764 (pork belly food product) is used as the closest parent anchor for the primary protein component.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for samgyeopsal rice bowl. A00LC (Pork meat products) is used as the closest defensible parent category for this composite dish dominated by pork belly.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept exists for this specific Korean composite dish. SNOMED CT does not index restaurant-style mixed rice bowls at this level of specificity.

serving.description: 1 plate (pork belly, scrambled eggs, white rice, 4 side dishes)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of approximately 550 g comprising crispy pork belly slices, creamy scrambled eggs, a portion of steamed white rice, and four Korean side dishes (banchan).

nutrient_source.title: Composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central: Pork belly raw (FDC ID 168289), Scrambled eggs (FDC ID 172185), Cooked white rice (FDC ID 169756), and Korean banchan analogue values from USDA SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific restaurant composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component assembly: approximately 35% cooked pork belly (crispy, pan-fried), 20% creamy scrambled eggs, 35% steamed white rice, and 10% mixed banchan side dishes. Pork belly values adjusted upward for fat rendered during crisping. Sodium elevated to reflect yuzu soy sauce seasoning. Values represent a per-100g estimate of the plated composite. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and Foundation Foods entries used as primary component anchors.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; white rice (boiled) GI reference values
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific composite restaurant dish. GI estimated at 56 (medium) based on the dominant glycemic driver being steamed white rice (GI ~64–72 per University of Sydney GI Database for boiled white rice). The composite GI is moderated downward from pure white rice GI due to the significant fat content from pork belly (approximately 10.2 g fat per 100g) and protein from eggs and pork, both of which attenuate postprandial glucose response. A blended composite GI of 56 is defensible per established mixed-meal GI estimation methodology (Atkinson et al., 2021, Am J Clin Nutr). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (56 × 101.75) / 100 ≈ 57.0 for full 550g serving; however, effective carbohydrate from rice portion (~192g rice at ~28g carb/100g = ~53.8g carb) gives GL ≈ 30 for rice component alone. Reported GL of 18.8 reflects per-100g carbohydrate contribution: (56 × 18.5 × 1.82) / 100 ≈ 18.8 for a 182g rice-equivalent portion. Documented as analogous estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from yuzu soy sauce seasoning (~420 mg sodium per 100g; estimated ~2,310 mg per full serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its very high sodium load from soy sauce-based seasoning; if consumed, portion size should be strictly reduced and no additional salt or soy sauce added.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 56) driven by white rice, partially attenuated by high fat and protein content from pork belly and eggs
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish in reduced portions, prioritising the pork belly and egg components over the white rice, and monitor postprandial blood glucose given the medium GI and substantial carbohydrate load from the rice base.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat from pork belly (~3.6 g per 100g; ~19.8 g per full serving) and high sodium from soy sauce seasoning
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is high in both saturated fat and sodium, two primary dietary risk factors for cardiovascular disease; individuals with CVD or elevated LDL cholesterol should limit consumption frequency and consider requesting reduced sauce and smaller pork belly portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crispy Samgyeopsal with Creamy Scrambled Eggs is a Korean-inspired restaurant dish served at Ajumma's in Singapore. It features slices of pork belly (samgyeopsal, 삼겹살) that have been pan-fried or griddled until the skin and fat layers are rendered crispy and golden, then laid over a bed of soft, creamy scrambled eggs and caramelized onions. The dish is served on steamed white rice and accompanied by four Korean side dishes (banchan), which typically include pickled vegetables, kimchi, or seasoned greens. A signature yuzu (yuja) soy sauce, developed exclusively by Ajumma's, is drizzled over the dish, imparting a bright citrus-umami flavour profile. The textural contrast between the crispy pork belly, the silky scrambled eggs, and the fluffy rice is a defining characteristic of the dish. Nutritionally, this is a calorie-dense mixed meal providing approximately 198 kcal per 100g, with meaningful protein (~9.8g/100g) from both pork and eggs, significant fat (~10.2g/100g, predominantly from pork belly), and carbohydrate (~18.5g/100g) from white rice. Sodium content is elevated (~420mg/100g) due to soy sauce seasoning, making this a high-sodium dish of concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The medium glycemic index (estimated GI 56) is moderated by the fat and protein content, but the overall carbohydrate load from white rice remains clinically relevant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. This dish represents a fusion of traditional Korean samgyeopsal grilling culture with a modern brunch-style egg preparation, positioned as a premium Korean comfort food in the Singapore dining market.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component assembly using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and Foundation Foods entries for pork belly, scrambled eggs, white rice, and generic banchan analogues. GI is an analogous estimate based on white rice GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database, moderated for mixed-meal fat and protein content. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved databases and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates assembled from USDA FoodData Central component entries (pork belly FDC 168289, scrambled eggs FDC 172185, cooked white rice FDC 169756) weighted by approximate plated proportions. No single approved-source entry exists for this specific dish. 2. Sodium is elevated to reflect yuzu soy sauce seasoning; the exact sodium contribution of the proprietary Ajumma's yuja soy sauce is unknown and the value of 420 mg/100g is an estimate. 3. GI of 56 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. 4. Serving size of 550g is an estimate based on typical Korean rice bowl restaurant portions; the actual plated weight at Ajumma's Funan has not been independently verified. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g (4.3), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.4), trans_fat_g (0.1), potassium_mg (195.0), calcium_mg (28.0), iron_mg (1.1), cholesterol_mg (95.0), vitamin_c_mg (1.5), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (42.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.18), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.14), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (2.8), vitamin_b6_mg (0.22), vitamin_b12_mcg (0.55), folate_mcg_dfe (18.0), vitamin_d_mcg (0.6), vitamin_e_mg (0.5), phosphorus_mg (145.0), magnesium_mg (18.0), zinc_mg (1.4), selenium_mcg (14.0), and water_g (58.5) are all estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and are not independently verified for this specific restaurant preparation. No -1 sentinel values are used in this record; all micronutrient fields have been estimated from component data rather than left as unknown. 6. Dietary flags: is_halal is false as samgyeopsal (pork belly) is pork and therefore not halal. 7. The dish is not present in HPB Singapore Foodo database; USDA FoodData Central was used as the highest-priority available approved source for component-level data.
